headlines.title: Public transportation guide for San Francisco's Pride parade and celebrations
headlines.description: San Francisco’s annual Pride celebration, one of the largest in the world, will kick off during the last weekend of June and transit agencies are ready to help make the experience as smooth as possible. The theme of the 56th annual San Francisco Pride will be “Resistance in Action.” The fair will be located at Civic Center Plaza on Saturday and Sunday, and the parade will march down Market Street on Sunday. The parade will begin at 10:30 a.m. at Embarcadero Plaza and will travel down Market Street before finishing at City Hall. Spectators can watch along the entire 1.6-mile-long route. BART has four stops along the parade route — Embarcadero, Montgomery, Powell and Civic Center stations. San Francisco 18 hours ago Giants News Jun 19 San Francisco Jun 5 Saturday will have normal service, but on Sunday the agency plans to help people “ride with pride” by opening at 8 a.m. and offering five-line service. Trains will be scheduled to arrive every five minutes at the downtown stations. After 9 p.m., service will be reduced to three lines. Special event trains will be run between Millbrae and downtown San Francisco, as well as from Pleasant Hill from 9:30 a.m. until 11 a.m., before restarting at 2 p.m. until the evening. Muni will be rerouting due to regular travel down Market Street onto Mission Street. Muni Metro light-rail vehicles will remain in service, allowing attendees to enter and exit at Embarcadero, Montgomery, Powell and Civic Center stations. Caltrain will be offering its half-hourly regular weekend service from 9:30 a.m. to 1 p.m. Riders can then transfer onto a Muni Metro line after arriving in the Downtown San Francisco Fourth and King station. The San Francisco Bay Ferry will be offering additional morning service from Vallejo, Oakland and Alameda alongside their regular weekend service. Riders traveling from Richmond, Alameda or Oakland are offered free parking at ferry-affiliated structures. For those traveling on buses, Alameda-Contra Costa Transit will be offering its regular weekend transbay services to the Salesforce Transit Center. Golden Gate Transit will be operating its buses and ferries on its regular weekend service. Those looking to arrive early are encouraged to take the 9 a.m. Larkspur ferry that will travel directly to the San Francisco Ferry Terminal. More information on the Pride celebration can be found at sfpride.org
headlines.source: Andres Jimenez Larios | Bay City News and Sophia Taylor

headlines.title: Adobe's third semaphore puzzle solved in San Jose after three years
headlines.description: One of the world’s most difficult puzzles created has been solved after three years. Brian Vincent, a San Jose software engineer, was able to crack the code for the third Adobe Semaphore puzzle, the tech giant announced. Vincent solved it this past spring after Adobe launched the creation in May 2023. The first puzzle was displayed in 2006, created by Ben Rubin, an artist who heavily incorporates science and technology into his work. Containing four large LED wheels that rotate and land at different axis positions, the puzzle draws audiences and can take years to solve. The first puzzle was solved in 2007 and the second in 2017. A fourth puzzle is in the making and will be open for anyone to attempt to solve. To hear more about Vincent’s solving strategies and Rubin’s creation of the puzzle, visit adobe.com/sj-semaphore.

headlines.title: World Cup in the Bay Area: Jordan vs. Algeria in Santa Clara
headlines.description: World Cup action returns to the Bay Area Monday night when Jordan takes on Algeria in group stage play. Kickoff for the Group J match at San Francisco Bay Area Stadium is scheduled for 8 p.m. Both nations came up short in their World Cup openers last Tuesday. Jordan fell to Austria, 3-1, while Algeria was blanked by Argentina, 3-0. Monday night’s clash will mark the fourth World Cup match at the South Bay stadium. One more group stage match – Paraguay vs. Australia – is scheduled for Thursday, followed by a Round of 32 contest featuring the United States on July 1. San Jose 18 hours ago 2026 World Cup Jun 17 San Jose Jun 20 Organizers to add 4th screen in San Pedro Square for World Cup watch parties

headlines.title: Person hospitalized after possibly struck by BART at Walnut Creek station
headlines.description: A person who may have been hit by a train Monday morning at BART’s Walnut Creek station has been hospitalized, officials said. The station was initially closed after the incident about 10:45 a.m. but has since reopened. The person reportedly entered the trackway as a train approached at and may have been hit, a BART spokesperson said. The injured person was taken to a hospital. Their condition was not released. This is a developing story. Please check back for updates.

headlines.title: California files lawsuit against Trump administration over Clean Air Act
headlines.description: California Attorney General Rob Bonta announced Monday that the state has filed a lawsuit against the Trump administration over its decision to reclassify four Clean Air Act preemption waivers. The waivers include the 2008 greenhouse gas emission standards waiver, the Advanced Clean Cars I (ACC I) waiver, the 2022 reinstatement of portions of the ACC I waiver and the 2022 Small Off-Road Engine rule amendment. The waivers allow California to implement emissions standards that are stricter than federal requirements. The Environmental Protection Agency originally granted the waivers as “orders.” The agency is now reclassifying those orders as “rules.” State officials argue the change carries significant consequences. “This may sound very technical, and it is, but it’s also a critical distinction. Orders aren’t subject to expedited congressional disapproval,” Bonta said. “Rules, however, are subject to expedited congressional disapproval.” Bonta argues the EPA lacks the legal authority to reclassify the waivers and is exceeding its authority in what he described as an effort to undermine California’s clean-air policies and environmental progress.

headlines.title: Bay Area temperatures expected to rise through midweek, ocean remains hazardous
headlines.description: A warming trend will build across the Bay Area and Central Coast through midweek, while dangerous beach conditions are expected to persist through Wednesday afternoon, according to the National Weather Service. Temps are expected to gradually climb through the middle of the week, with the warmest conditions arriving on Wednesday. Pockets of Moderate HeatRisk are possible in the Santa Clara Valley, where highs could reach the 80s and lower 90s. The risk is greatest for people sensitive to heat, including older adults, children, people with health conditions and those without adequate cooling or hydration, forecasters said Monday. While inland temps rise, the weather service is also warning beachgoers about a long-period southerly swell that is increasing the risk of sneaker waves and strong rip currents along the Pacific coast. The greatest threat is expected at south- and southwest-facing beaches from the North Bay to Monterey County. Forecasters urged visitors to stay off jetties, piers and shoreline rocks, and reminded anyone heading to the coast to never turn their back on the ocean. The Beach Hazards Statement remains in effect through Wednesday afternoon.

headlines.title: San Jose considers extending parking meter hours, doubling rates downtown
headlines.description: A night out in downtown San Jose could soon cost drivers more. The city is considering a proposal that would extend parking meter hours and increase parking rates as officials look to capitalize on increased nightlife activity downtown. Under the proposal, metered parking would be enforced until 9 p.m. instead of 6 p.m. The hourly rate would also double from $2 to $4. City officials say about 1,600 parking meters would be affected. Some meters, including those in Japantown and east of City Hall, would be exempt from the changes. The proposed $4 hourly rate would match the cost of parking in several city-owned garages. San Jose’s smart meters allow drivers to pay by tapping a card or paying through a mobile app, eliminating the need to carry coins or cash. The city estimates extending meter hours and increasing rates could generate about $1.2 million in additional annual revenue.

headlines.title: Man dies after San Jose e-scooter crash, police say
headlines.description: A man injured in an electric scooter crash in San Jose has died, marking the city’s 22nd traffic-related fatality of 2026, police said. The crash occurred at about 4:56 p.m. June 9 near Commercial Street and Oakland Road, San Jose police noted. According to a preliminary investigation by the San Jose Police Department, the rider was traveling eastbound in a westbound bike lane on a black Segway electric scooter when he lost control at a high rate of speed and fell. Police said the rider was not wearing a helmet. He was taken to a local hospital with life-threatening injuries and later stabilized. Investigators learned Tuesday that the man died from his injuries on June 13, police said. The Santa Clara County Medical Examiner’s Office will release the victim’s identity. Anyone with information about the crash is asked to contact the Detective Banzon of the San Jose Police Department’s Traffic Investigations Unit at: 4661@sanjoseca.gov or (408) 277-4654.

headlines.title: Stolen memorial monument from Vietnamese Heritage Garden in San Jose recovered
headlines.description: Authorities recovered a memorial monument that was stolen from the Vietnamese Heritage Garden in San Jose Sunday afternoon. Police said some suspects stole the memorial monument from the garden located in Kelley Park on Roberts Avenue. With the help of a neighborhood patrol unit, officers later found the statue leaning against a rocky area. Investigators were trying to figure out how the thieves stole the statue and why.

headlines.title: Fans pack San Pedro Square for Spain-Saudi Arabia World Cup watch party
headlines.description: San Jose’s San Pedro Square continues to be a hotspot for World Cup watch parties. Sunday, soccer fans packed the plaza to watch the Spain-Saudi Arabia match. Fans were proudly wearing their team colors from head to toe. The game was exciting, with a lot of action in the first half. Marianne Favro has the full report in the video above.

headlines.title: Lasers will light up the sky over San Francisco for Pride, World Cup, July 4
headlines.description: Starting Sunday night, multicolor lasers will beam light into the sky over downtown San Francisco as part of a new public art project called 7×7. This display will run overnight from June 21 through July 4 and is intended to celebrate San Francisco’s Pride festival, the FIFA World Cup, and America’s 250th birthday celebration on the 4th of July. The display is slated to run every night from sunset to sunrise. These lasers were organized by the nonprofit Illuminate, which also leads the other major light displays in the city such as the Bay Bridge lights and the Market Street Pride Flag lights. San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie’s Office also collaborated on bringing this project forward. This display will be projected from 49 cannons (representing the 49 square miles of San Francisco), pointing towards the sky from the city’s Civic Center plaza. Illuminate founder Ben Davis explained that the lasers will be rainbow colors to celebrate Pride in San Francisco, and on the Fourth of July, they will be red, white, and blue. But in between those times, he said organizers may experiment with other colors and patterns. Based on how other laser displays have gone in San Francisco, Davis expects people will be able to see this light display from miles away, even as far as mountaintops across the bay. He said that how visible the lasers are may come down to how the fog behaves, and organizers will learn more through the different weather patterns that unfold over the next 14 days.

headlines.title: NWS warns of hazardous conditions along Northern California coastline
headlines.description: The entire Northern California coastline is under beach hazard warnings. The National Weather Service warns that, for the next few days, the danger will be higher than normal. San Francisco’s Baker Beach is notorious for having exceptionally strong sneaker waves. The danger of those waves will be higher than normal, according to the NWS. Santa Cruz State Parks lifeguards said teams have been busy with water rescues for more than a week. Two Bay Area college students in their 20s were napping on a beach near Santa Cruz on June 10 when they were swept out to sea by a sneaker wave and drowned. On Thursday, a mother and a child were pulled from the water, after they were hit by a sneaker wave at Baker Beach. At last report, they were hospitalized in critical condition with non-life-threatening injuries. San Francisco Jun 18 Santa Cruz County Jun 16 Experienced Bay Area crabber Volark Keo is a moderator for the Pacifica Pier Fishing and Crabbing group. He is charge of warning crabbers about the surf dangers in social media advisories. “Basically, respect the wave. For me, I pray before during and after, and always keep my head on a swivel, no matter what. And if you have a bad feeling, get out. Crab another day. Live another day,” he said. The National Weather Service advises beach goers to stay far from the water’s edge, and off jetties, rocks and piers, and as always never turn their backs on the ocean. The NWS says the Surf Hazard Advisories will remain in effect until at least Wednesday evening.
